title: Aerobic respiration — releasing energy using oxygen
examTip: AQA tests the word equation, the symbol equation, and where respiration happens. Make sure you can distinguish respiration from breathing, and link mitochondria to cells with high energy demands.
introTips: This lesson starts by separating respiration from breathing — they're related but completely different things, and confusing them is one of the most common errors in exams.
You'll learn the word and symbol equations for aerobic respiration — both are required at GCSE.
We'll also look at WHERE respiration happens and why that matters for certain types of cells.
intro: Every cell in your body is running on glucose, right now, as you read this. Your heart is beating, your brain is firing, your eyes are moving across this screen — all of it powered by a chemical reaction happening inside each cell. That reaction is respiration. It's not breathing. It's not lungs. It's a molecular process that happens in mitochondria, releasing energy from glucose. Once you understand that, a huge amount of biology starts to make sense.

keyPoints.heading: Respiration is NOT breathing
keyPoints.content: This is the most important distinction in the topic. Respiration is a chemical reaction that takes place in every living cell. It releases energy from glucose. It happens whether you're asleep, awake, exercising or completely still. Breathing (ventilation) is the physical process of moving air in and out of your lungs — it delivers oxygen to the blood and removes carbon dioxide. The two are related because respiration needs oxygen (in aerobic respiration) and produces CO₂, so breathing supplies the inputs and removes the outputs. But they are fundamentally different: one is a chemical reaction inside cells; the other is a mechanical movement of the chest. In an exam, never write 'respiration is breathing' — it is not.
writeBullets: Respiration = chemical reaction in cells that releases energy from glucose
Happens in ALL living cells, ALL the time (not just during exercise)
Breathing (ventilation) = mechanical process of moving air in and out of lungs
Connection: breathing supplies O₂ for respiration and removes CO₂ produced by respiration
They are related but completely different processes

keyTerms.def: The chemical reaction in cells that releases energy from glucose using oxygen, producing carbon dioxide and water
The mechanical process of breathing — moving air into and out of the lungs to supply oxygen and remove carbon dioxide
The sugar molecule used as fuel in respiration — obtained from digestion of carbohydrates

keyPoints.memTip: Respiration happens in a cell. Breathing happens in a chest. One is chemistry, one is mechanics. If a question asks about respiration, your answer should mention glucose, oxygen, energy, mitochondria — not lungs, diaphragm or chest.

keyPoints.heading: The word and symbol equations
keyPoints.content: Aerobic respiration in words: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water (+ energy released). In symbols: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O. The energy released is captured as ATP (adenosine triphosphate) — the cell's energy currency. ATP is used wherever energy is needed: to contract muscles, to pump ions across membranes, to build large molecules. The equation is balanced: 6 carbons in, 6 carbons out (as CO₂). 12 hydrogens in glucose plus 12 from the 6 water molecules — it all balances. You need to be able to write both versions from memory. Note that AQA expects you to know ATP as the product — not just 'energy'.
writeBullets: Word equation: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water (+ energy as ATP)
Symbol equation: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O
Energy is released as ATP — the cell's usable energy currency
Equation is balanced: 6C in, 6CO₂ out; all atoms accounted for
Learn both versions — exams ask for either one

keyTerms.def: Adenosine triphosphate — the molecule that stores and transfers energy inside cells; produced by respiration
A waste product of aerobic respiration — produced when carbon from glucose is oxidised and exhaled via the lungs
A chemical equation where the number of atoms of each element is the same on both sides — no atoms are created or destroyed

keyPoints.memTip: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O. The '6s' are your anchor: 6 oxygen molecules in, 6 CO₂ out, 6 water out. If you can write those three 6s and the glucose formula, the rest follows. Practise writing it from scratch three times — don't just read it.

keyPoints.heading: Where respiration happens — mitochondria
keyPoints.content: Aerobic respiration takes place in the mitochondria — membrane-bound organelles found in almost all eukaryotic cells. The inner membrane is folded into structures called cristae, which increase the surface area for the reactions. Because respiration is where ATP is made, cells with high energy demands have more mitochondria. Muscle cells contract constantly and need huge amounts of ATP — they are packed with mitochondria. Liver cells are metabolically very active (they process toxins, synthesise proteins, regulate blood glucose) and also have very high mitochondria counts. Sperm cells have mitochondria concentrated in the mid-piece to power the tail. If a question asks why a cell has many mitochondria, the answer always links to energy demand.
writeBullets: Aerobic respiration occurs in the MITOCHONDRIA
Mitochondria have a folded inner membrane (cristae) — increases surface area for reactions
More mitochondria = higher energy demand
Muscle cells: lots of mitochondria (constant contraction needs ATP)
Liver cells: lots of mitochondria (high metabolic activity)
Sperm cells: mitochondria in mid-piece (power the flagellum)

keyTerms.def: Organelles found in almost all eukaryotic cells where aerobic respiration takes place and ATP is produced
Folds in the inner membrane of a mitochondrion that increase the surface area for aerobic respiration reactions
The energy-carrying molecule produced in the mitochondria during respiration — used to power cellular processes

keyPoints.memTip: Always link mitochondria to energy demand. If a question shows you a cell packed with mitochondria and asks why, the answer is always 'this cell requires a lot of energy (ATP) for...' and then name the function. Muscle contraction, active transport, protein synthesis — any high-energy job means more mitochondria.

keyPoints.heading: What the energy from respiration is used for
keyPoints.content: The ATP produced by aerobic respiration powers four main types of work in the body. Muscle contraction: actin and myosin filaments require ATP to slide past each other — every heartbeat, every breath, every movement. Active transport: pumping ions and molecules against their concentration gradient across cell membranes requires energy input — for example, in the gut absorbing glucose, or in the kidney reabsorbing ions. Synthesis of large molecules: building proteins from amino acids, or making DNA, or assembling glycogen — all require energy to form chemical bonds. Maintaining body temperature: in mammals and birds, some of the energy from respiration is released as heat to keep the body at a constant warm temperature (37 °C in humans), which keeps enzymes working near their optimum.
writeBullets: Muscle contraction — ATP powers the sliding of muscle fibres
Active transport — pumping molecules against concentration gradients
Synthesis of large molecules — making proteins, DNA, glycogen
Maintaining body temperature — heat from respiration keeps enzymes at optimum

keyTerms.def: The movement of molecules against their concentration gradient across a cell membrane, requiring energy (ATP) from respiration
The building of large molecules from smaller ones — requires energy from ATP
The maintenance of a constant body temperature — partly achieved using heat released by respiration in mammals and birds

keyPoints.memTip: Four uses of ATP: Muscles Move, Active transport, Synthesis, Temperature. MAST — like a ship's mast. Muscles, Active transport, Synthesis, Temperature. If you can write those four and explain one example each, you'll handle any question on this.

1.question: A student says 'respiration is the same as breathing'. What is wrong with this statement?
options: Nothing — respiration and breathing are the same process
Respiration only happens during exercise, whereas breathing is continuous
Respiration is a chemical reaction in cells that releases energy; breathing is a mechanical process that moves air in and out of the lungs
Respiration happens in the lungs; breathing happens in every cell
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Respiration is a chemical reaction inside every cell, releasing energy from glucose. Breathing (ventilation) is the mechanical movement of air in and out of the lungs. They are related but completely different processes.
1.wrongFeedback: Respiration and breathing are NOT the same. Respiration is a chemical reaction that happens in every living cell (including when you're asleep) to release energy from glucose. Breathing is the physical movement of the chest that ventilates the lungs. They're connected — breathing supplies oxygen for respiration — but they are different things.

2.question: Which of the following is the correct symbol equation for aerobic respiration?
2.correctFeedback: Correct — C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O. Glucose and oxygen are the reactants on the left; carbon dioxide and water are the products on the right.
2.wrongFeedback: The correct equation is C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O. Option B is photosynthesis written backwards. Make sure you know which side the reactants (glucose + oxygen) are on and which side the products (CO₂ + water) are on.

commonMistakes: Writing 'respiration is breathing' — they are completely different processes. Respiration is a chemical reaction inside cells; breathing is the mechanical movement of air in and out of the lungs.

Leaving ATP out of the equation and just writing 'energy' — AQA mark schemes at GCSE expect you to know that the energy is released as ATP. Get the habit of writing '+ ATP' rather than '+ energy released'.

Writing the symbol equation with the wrong number of molecules — the common error is using 6H₂O on the left (that's photosynthesis). In aerobic respiration, water is a PRODUCT on the right, not a reactant.
Saying respiration happens 'in the nucleus' or 'in the cytoplasm' — the main stage of aerobic respiration is in the mitochondria. Specify the organelle.
Stating that cells with more mitochondria 'produce more glucose' — mitochondria produce ATP from glucose; they don't make glucose. More mitochondria = more ATP production capacity.
examTips: When asked to write the equation for aerobic respiration, write the word equation first then the symbol equation — if you make an error in the symbols, the word equation may still earn you marks.

For questions asking why a particular cell has many mitochondria, always structure your answer as: 'Cell X requires a lot of energy (ATP) for [specific function] — this means it needs many mitochondria to produce enough ATP through aerobic respiration.'

If asked to distinguish aerobic from anaerobic respiration, the key differences are: aerobic uses oxygen and produces CO₂ and water; anaerobic produces lactic acid (animals) or ethanol + CO₂ (yeast) and releases far less energy.

revisionCardBullets: Respiration = chemical reaction in cells releasing energy from glucose — NOT the same as breathing
Happens in ALL cells, ALL the time (not just during exercise)
Word equation: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water (+ ATP)
Energy is released as ATP — the cell's usable energy currency
Location: mitochondria — folded inner membrane (cristae) gives large surface area
More mitochondria = higher energy demand (muscle cells, liver cells, sperm mid-piece)
Four uses of ATP: Muscles, Active transport, Synthesis of large molecules, Temperature (MAST)
Respiration vs breathing: respiration = chemistry inside cells; breathing = mechanics in the chest
